hq: Not disclosed (no address in report); website: tryturnstile.co

system_description.overview: Turnstile is an AI-powered revenue platform for B2B SaaS companies that automates and streamlines quoting, subscription management, billing, and financial reporting. The platform replaces manual spreadsheet-based revenue operations with automated workflows integrating with CRM and ERP systems.

products.Quoting: E-sign capabilities, instant checkout, automated quote generation
products.Subscription Management: 360-degree customer insights, contract amendments, provisioning, renewal optimization
products.Billing Automation: Real-time invoicing, usage-based billing, automated billing workflows
products.Financial Reporting: ARR/MRR metrics, revenue recognition, financial analytics

infrastructure.cloud_provider: Amazon Web Services (AWS) — carve-out method (subservice organization with its own SOC 2 report)
regions: us-east-2 (Ohio) — confirmed from subnet labels US-EAST-2A, US-EAST-2B in network diagram
infrastructure.physical_presence: No physical offices; no physical servers
infrastructure.availability_zones: Multi-AZ (spins up multiple servers across all availability zones in AWS on AZ failure)

network_architecture.network_segmentation: Two VPCs: Development VPC (TURNSTILE-DEV-ACCT) and Production VPC (TURNSTILE-MAIN-VPC) with 2 AZs each (US-EAST-2A and US-EAST-2B), each AZ having public and private subnets (CDR ~10.50.0.0). Managed via AWS IAM Organizations structure: ROOT ACCOUNT (IAM Organizations + Management, Consolidated Billing) with ACCESS GROUPS (humans, admins, contractors). Customer accounts appropriately segmented from each other.
network_architecture.firewalls: System firewalls configured on application gateway and production network to limit unnecessary ports, protocols and services; reviewed annually by IT management. Formal network diagram outlining boundary protection mechanisms (firewalls, IDS, etc.) maintained for all network connections and reviewed annually by IT management (CC2.3.4).
network_architecture.ids_ips: Firewalls and intrusion detection tools configured to prevent unauthorized access and detect external security threats (CC6.6.1). IDS and IPS via AWS.
network_architecture.vpn: Remote access via bastion host with VPN
network_architecture.tls: Encryption technologies used to protect communication and transmission of data over public networks (CC6.1.6)
network_architecture.content_filtering: Content filtering system routed through proxy server; email scanning to prevent malicious scripts
network_architecture.topology: ROOT ACCOUNT (IAM Organizations, Management, Consolidated Billing, ACCESS GROUPS: humans/admins/contractors) -> ROOT DNS ZONE (tryturnstile.co) -> NETWORK ACCOUNT -> Development VPC (TURNSTILE-DEV-ACCT, us-east-2, 2 AZs with public+private subnets) + Production VPC (TURNSTILE-MAIN-VPC, us-east-2, 2 AZs with public+private subnets, NAT Gateway, Internet Gateway, CDR ~10.50.0.0)

application_architecture.pattern: Not fully disclosed; cloud-native on AWS with separate dev/prod environments
compute: EC2 (implied from VPC/subnet architecture with public and private subnets)
application_architecture.environments: Separate development and production environments; changes tested before migration to production (CC8.1.4)

databases: AWS (implied; specific service not named — RDS likely given private subnets in network diagram)
data_storage.backup_frequency: Not specified (backup policy exists; Turnstile spins up multiple servers across all AZs in AWS on failover — BCDR described as logical controls for AZ-level failover)

authentication_access_control.identity_provider: AWS (Active Directory for server/database accounts; IAM Organizations for infrastructure access groups: humans, admins, contractors)
authentication_access_control.mfa: MFA enforced for all administrative access to production platform (CC6.1.1); MFA required to access source code management tool (CC8.1.3); 2FA for employee email
authentication_access_control.access_revocation_sla: Within one business day upon termination (CC6.1.4) — ticketing checklist used
authentication_access_control.password_policy: AWS password policy with complexity requirements; unique user IDs required for application production environment, Active Directory, server, and database accounts (CC6.1.2). Password settings in accordance with corresponding password requirements defined in policy.

encryption.at_rest: Cloud provider key management service (AWS KMS) used to encrypt data at rest (CC6.1.5)
encryption.key_management: AWS KMS; access to production environment access keys restricted to authorized individuals

ci_cd_devops.source_control: Not named (source code management tool with MFA access; branch rules configured — CC8.1.3). Source code changes logged, time-stamped, and attributed to author.
ci_cd_devops.ci_cd_tool: Not named; changes tracked in internal tool from initiation through deployment and validation (CC8.1.1)
ci_cd_devops.branch_protection: Code repository branch rules configured to require additional approval for every merge request to move change to production environment (CC8.1.2). Developers cannot make changes to production code without additional approval.
ci_cd_devops.iac: Configuration management tool used for implementation and rollback; baseline configurations retained as code in infrastructure (CC7.1.1). Reviewed and updated annually or when required.
ci_cd_devops.patch_management: Automatic patch management set up by CTO/Management; monthly patch management reviews; workstations configured for automatic updates and scanned daily for patch compliance (CC7.5.1)

monitoring_logging.apm_tool: Not named (monitoring tools for capacity/anomaly detection; monitoring tool generates alerts when predefined thresholds are met — CC5.2.1)
monitoring_logging.log_aggregation: AWS logging (implied); logging enabled for administrative activities, logon attempts, data deletions at application and infrastructure level, changes to functions, security configurations, permissions, and roles (CC7.2.1). Access to change log configuration and modify logs is restricted.
monitoring_logging.alerting: Automated alerts configured to notify IT management of high and critical risk events; issues resolved in timely manner through incident management process (CC7.2.1)

third_party_services.purpose: Primary cloud infrastructure subservice — compute, VPC networking, KMS, IAM Organizations, multi-AZ

security_tools.antivirus: Anti-virus and Malware protection software installed and configured on all production servers (continuous auto-scan; definitions updated weekly). Anti-virus and Malware protection software installed and configured for Mac based workstations and laptops (CC6.8.1).
security_tools.vulnerability_scanning: Monthly automated vulnerability scans on infrastructure and applications; management reviews and takes action on high/critical findings; remediation plan developed; resolution follows incident response plan (CC7.1.2)
security_tools.penetration_testing: Annual external penetration testing by independent third-party (CC4.1.1)

bcdr.rto: Not disclosed (business impact analysis referenced; time frames identified in DR plan)

excluded_criteria: CC6.4 — Physical access to data center; AWS is responsible for physical security (carve-out); SOC 2 report of AWS reviewed annually (CC6.4.1)
A1.2 — Environmental protections and software/data backup recovery infrastructure; managed by AWS/building management
Processing Integrity — determined not relevant to the system
Privacy — determined not relevant to the system
notable_controls: CC2.1.4: Defined roles/responsibilities for CISO assigned to oversee information security policies and incident response
CC2.3.4: Formal network diagram maintained for all network connections, reviewed annually
CC4.1.1: Annual external penetration testing by independent third-party
CC5.2.1: IT team continuously monitors capacity and performance; alerting on predefined thresholds
CC6.1.1: MFA enforced for all administrative access to production platform
CC6.1.2: Unique user IDs and passwords required for application, AD, server, and database accounts
CC6.1.4: Access revoked within one business day of termination via checklist
CC6.1.5: AWS KMS used to encrypt data at rest
CC6.4.1: All production systems in cloud; AWS SOC 2 report reviewed annually
CC6.5.1: Formal data retention and disposal policy; customer data retained per contractual agreement post-termination
CC6.6.1: Firewalls and IDS deployed to prevent unauthorized access and detect external threats
CC7.1.1: Server hardening; baseline configurations retained as code with rollback capability
CC7.1.2: Monthly automated vulnerability scanning; high/critical findings remediated
CC7.4.1: BCDR policy tested annually across multiple loss scenarios including AZ-level failover
CC8.1.1: Formal change management policy; emergency change process with accelerated timeline
CC8.1.2: Branch protection — no production changes without additional approval
CC8.1.3: Source code management with MFA; changes logged and attributed to author
CC8.1.4: Separate dev/test environments; changes tested before promotion to production
CC9.1: Annual SOC report reviews of AWS; quarterly vendor risk assessments for non-SOC vendors (CC9.2.1)
CC9.2.2: Vendor security commitments documented and agreed during onboarding
CC9.2.3: Vendor termination process with access revocation checklist

infrastructure.rationale: AWS us-east-2 with multi-AZ (2 AZs for production VPC), separate Development and Production VPCs, IAM Organizations with structured access groups, IaC (config-as-code), NAT Gateway, Internet Gateway. No CDN mentioned, no multi-region, single cloud provider. Actual and customized network diagram. Root DNS zone managed in AWS (tryturnstile.co).

application_architecture.rationale: Separate dev/prod environments, branch protection, code reviews, config-as-code. No languages or frameworks disclosed. No container orchestration. Application architecture details beyond the VPC/subnet layout not revealed. Revenue platform with CRM/ERP integrations but no tech stack specifics disclosed.

data_layer.rationale: Database service not specifically named (AWS managed DB implied from private subnets). No caching layer mentioned. No data warehouse. Backup frequency not specified; DR relies on AZ-level failover via AWS multi-AZ. Data classification and retention policies in place. Per-tenant segregation confirmed.

security_posture.rationale: Annual external pen testing, monthly vuln scans, IDS/IPS via AWS, firewalls, MFA for admin access and source code, antivirus on servers (weekly definitions) and Mac endpoints, quarterly access reviews, 1-day revocation SLA, AWS KMS encryption, content filtering via proxy, email scanning, CISO role assigned. No WAF named, no SIEM named, no cybersecurity insurance.

devops_maturity.rationale: Branch protection with mandatory approval for production merges, separate environments, emergency change process with accelerated approval timeline, config-as-code (unnamed tool), automatic patch management, monthly reviews, internal change tracking tool. CI/CD tool not named, IaC tool not named, source control tool not named.

bcdr_readiness.rationale: BCDR policy documented, reviewed and tested annually across multiple loss scenarios. Multi-AZ failover via AWS. Business impact analysis referenced. Roles/responsibilities and time frames defined in DR plan. No RTO/RPO disclosed, no multi-region failover, backup frequency not specified.

vendor_diversity.rationale: Single critical vendor: AWS (compute, networking, KMS, IAM). No secondary cloud, CDN, or additional managed services identified. Extreme AWS concentration risk — entire stack on a single cloud provider in a single region (us-east-2).

overall.rationale: Solid security hygiene for a SOC 2 Type 1 report with well-structured AWS account architecture (IAM Organizations, separate VPCs, proper access group hierarchy). Strong access controls and a genuine, customized network diagram. Key weaknesses: Security trust criteria only, single cloud provider entirely concentrated in AWS us-east-2, no RTO/RPO, backup frequency unspecified, no WAF or SIEM named, no CDN, entire tech stack (languages, frameworks, databases) undisclosed.

red_flags: No RTO or RPO disclosed — BCDR maturity cannot be quantified despite multi-AZ setup and business impact analysis
No WAF named — web application attack surface for a B2B SaaS revenue platform unclear
No SIEM tool named — security event correlation capability unknown
CI/CD tool, IaC tool, and source control tool names not disclosed — tooling quality unverifiable
No database service specifically named — storage layer quality and redundancy unverifiable
Security trust criteria only — Availability, Confidentiality, Processing Integrity, and Privacy not covered
yellow_flags: Single cloud provider (AWS us-east-2 only) — regional AWS outage would impact service
No CDN mentioned — performance for distributed customers may be limited
No caching layer disclosed — potential performance concern at scale
Backup frequency not specified — data recovery exposure unknown
No multi-region failover — entire DR relies on AZ-level redundancy within us-east-2
Application languages and frameworks not disclosed — code quality unverifiable
green_flags: Annual external penetration testing by independent third-party (CC4.1.1)
Monthly automated vulnerability scanning with high/critical remediation tracking
MFA enforced for all administrative access, source code management, and employee email (2FA)
Quarterly user access reviews including privileged accounts
Access revocation within one business day of termination via checklist
BCDR plan tested annually with multiple loss scenarios including AZ-level failover
Branch protection with mandatory approval for all production merges (CC8.1.2)
Separate development and production environments with testing gate (CC8.1.4)
IDS/IPS active via AWS with firewalls on application gateway and production network
Antivirus on production servers (weekly definitions) and Mac workstations/laptops
Content filtering via proxy server with email scanning
Zero exceptions across all 29 tested controls
AWS SOC 2 report reviewed annually; quarterly vendor risk assessments for non-SOC vendors
Vendor security commitments documented during onboarding; termination checklist for access revocation
Structured IAM Organizations architecture with explicit access groups (humans/admins/contractors)
Emergency change process with documented accelerated approval timeline (CC8.1.1)
key_observations: Turnstile has an unusually well-structured AWS account hierarchy for a startup: ROOT ACCOUNT with IAM Organizations, separate NETWORK ACCOUNT, and explicit ACCESS GROUPS (humans, admins, contractors) — indicating deliberate infrastructure design rather than ad-hoc growth
The network diagram is genuine and customized (not a template placeholder) — shows actual two-VPC architecture (TURNSTILE-DEV-ACCT and TURNSTILE-MAIN-VPC) with subnet-level detail in us-east-2

Security is the only trust criteria — the decision to exclude Availability, Confidentiality, Processing Integrity, and Privacy is notable for a revenue management platform that processes financial data (ARR/MRR, invoicing, billing)
The CISO role is formally assigned (CC2.1.4) — unusual for a startup of this apparent size, suggesting either board/investor pressure or enterprise customer requirements

Turnstile's product (quoting, subscription management, billing automation, financial reporting) means it likely handles sensitive financial and contractual data — the exclusion of Confidentiality and Privacy trust criteria from scope warrants attention in due diligence
BCDR relies entirely on multi-AZ spinning up within AWS us-east-2 — no multi-region failover means a full AWS regional outage would bring down the service

The DR section (Section 5, p.56) explicitly notes that AICPA guidance prevents including DR controls in the SOC 2 controls section — they are presented as 'Other Information' only, meaning DR procedures are unaudited supplementary information

Jordan Zamir (CFO & Co-Founder) signed the management assertion — the CFO signing (rather than CEO) suggests Jordan owns the compliance function, which may reflect a finance-led compliance culture appropriate for a revenue management platform

Org chart includes VP Engineering (Ben Clay), Tech Lead Manager (Ryan Wilson), and Product Managers (Fiona Hasbrouck, James Malone) — suggesting a more mature engineering organization than a typical early-stage startup
Vendor management is notably mature: three separate CC9.x controls (annual SOC reviews, onboarding security commitments, termination procedures) plus quarterly risk assessments for non-SOC vendors
